Q1: I tried to keep a consistent routine, but honestly it was hard with everything going on. I usually studied in the evenings after my part-time job, maybe three to four hours on weekdays. I'm a visual learner so I spent a lot of time making diagrams and color-coded notes from the lecture videos. Weekends were my heavy study days since I had more time, and I'd dedicate most of Saturday to catching up on materials I'd fallen behind on during the week.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied depending on deadlines and how stressed I was feeling. I tried to have regular times, but life kept getting in the way—work shifts would change, or I'd have a rough week emotionally and need to adjust. When assignments were due, I'd shift into overdrive and study way more intensely. The weeks without major deadlines, I'd still study but maybe not as rigidly.
Q3: Usually it was checking if there were new announcements or if grades had been posted. Sometimes I'd log in because I remembered there was a video I needed to watch or a discussion I should contribute to. Honestly, a lot of times it was anxiety-driven—I'd wake up worried I'd missed something important and just needed to check. The notification emails also prompted me to log in when new content dropped.
Q4: Yeah, after the first few weeks it became pretty automatic to check the LMS in the morning before work and again at night. I think it happened because I was scared of falling behind—I've worked too hard to get here to mess it up now. The mindfulness exercises they introduced actually helped me approach it more calmly rather than out of panic. It became less stressful once it was just part of my day.
Q5: The lecture videos were everything for me since I learn best by seeing things explained visually. I'd watch them multiple times, pausing to draw out concepts and make my own visual summaries. The discussion forums were also helpful because reading how other students understood things gave me different perspectives. When someone posted a diagram or chart, that was gold for me.
Q6: I tended to skip the supplementary readings, especially the long PDFs without any visuals. With limited time and resources, I had to prioritize what would help me most efficiently. Some of the text-heavy forum threads I'd skim rather than read deeply. It wasn't that they weren't valuable, I just couldn't afford to spend hours on things that weren't directly tied to assignments.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see a direct connection to either understanding the material better or improving my grade. If it had visual elements or could be turned into something visual, I was more engaged. I also valued activities where I could see my progress or get feedback. Time is precious when you're juggling everything I'm juggling, so efficiency matters a lot.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't always afford. Required ones got my attention first, no question. But sometimes the optional stuff was actually more interesting or helpful—I just had to be strategic about when I could fit them in. If I had a lighter week, I'd explore optional materials, but during crunch time they were the first thing cut.
Q9: Absolutely. I downloaded almost everything so I could study offline—my internet isn't always reliable and I can't always afford data. I'd print diagrams at the library and study from my handwritten notes on the bus to work. I also used YouTube videos to supplement topics I didn't fully understand from the lectures. A lot of my actual learning happened away from any screen.
Q10: Most of my deep learning happened with printed materials and hand-drawn diagrams spread across my desk. I'd spend hours reorganizing concepts visually, connecting ideas with arrows and colors, but none of that shows up anywhere in the system. When I was memorizing for exams, I'd pace around my room talking through concepts out loud. The LMS would just show I downloaded a file once, not the ten hours I spent with it afterward.
Q11: All the time. I'd download a video or PDF once, then work with it for days or even weeks offline. My study style involves repetition and transformation—watching something, then recreating it in my own visual format. The LMS only captured that initial download, not the multiple times I rewatched on my laptop offline or the notes I generated from it.
Q12: There were definitely low points, especially mid-semester when stress hit hard. I was dealing with some personal stuff that left me feeling pretty sad and drained. Work hours increased around the same time, and I felt like I was drowning. The mindfulness intervention actually helped me push through—it gave me tools to manage the anxiety instead of letting it paralyze me.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted from work or feeling overwhelmed, I'd log in but barely absorb anything. There were weeks where competing responsibilities meant I could only do the bare minimum on the LMS. Confusion about certain topics made me avoid those sections until I absolutely had to face them. Fatigue especially affected how deeply I engaged—sometimes I was just going through the motions.
Q14: Yeah, there were times I'd have a video playing but my mind was somewhere else entirely. I'd be stressed about bills or tired from work, and I'd realize I'd watched twenty minutes without processing any of it. I'd have to rewatch later when I could actually focus. The system would show I watched it, but the first time barely counted as real engagement.
Q15: I definitely postponed some of the harder assignments, even knowing they were important. When you're already stressed and sad, facing something difficult feels impossible. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow when I felt better, but sometimes tomorrow wasn't much better. The mindfulness practice helped me recognize this pattern and push through it more often, but it still happened.
Q16: Deadlines basically dictated my entire LMS usage pattern. I'd be most active in the days leading up to due dates, sometimes staying up late to finish things. Without a deadline, I'd let things slide even if I knew I should stay ahead. It's not ideal, but when you're managing so much, deadlines create the urgency needed to prioritize coursework over everything else demanding your attention.
Q17: Feedback motivated me a lot, especially positive feedback. When I got good marks on an assignment, it energized me to engage more with the next one. Critical feedback was harder to receive when I was already feeling down, but I tried to use it constructively. Sometimes waiting for feedback made me anxious and I'd check the LMS obsessively until grades were posted.
Q18: The course structure with clear weekly modules helped me stay organized despite everything. I appreciated knowing what was expected each week so I could plan around work. The regular rhythm of content release gave me something predictable in a life that often feels chaotic. Having deadlines spread throughout rather than all at the end made it more manageable for someone in my situation.
Q19: The video-based content made engagement much easier for me as a visual learner. What made it harder was when instructions were unclear or buried in long text documents—I'd waste time just figuring out what I was supposed to do. The discussion forums were helpful but sometimes felt like extra work when I was already stretched thin. More visual summaries of key concepts would have helped a lot.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is trying hard but struggling to complete things to a standard they're satisfied with. Maybe they're exploring materials, attempting drafts, but not finishing. Or they could be dealing with personal issues that make it hard to follow through even when they're putting in effort. High activity doesn't always mean productive activity—sometimes it's anxious searching without real progress.
Q21: That actually describes me somewhat. I did a lot of learning offline that wouldn't show up in my activity logs. If someone looked at my login frequency, they might think I wasn't engaged, but I was spending hours with downloaded materials. Some students are just efficient—they know exactly what they need and don't waste time clicking around. Performance and visible activity aren't the same thing.
Q22: Course logs can accurately show when someone accessed materials and submitted work—the basic timeline of engagement. But they miss the quality and depth of that engagement entirely. They can't capture offline studying, emotional investment, or the struggle behind each submission. They especially miss students like me who download once and study extensively offline.
Q23: Being engaged means genuinely caring about understanding the material, not just completing tasks. It's when you find yourself thinking about course concepts outside of study time, making connections to your life. For me, it's when I'm actively transforming information into visual formats that make sense to me. It's pushing through even when things are hard because you're invested in your own growth.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I've had to overcome just to be here—the financial struggles, the emotional weight, the exhaustion from balancing work and school. It wouldn't capture the resilience it takes to keep going when you're feeling sad and stressed. It can't measure how the mindfulness intervention genuinely helped my mental state, or how proud I am of earning a Merit despite everything. My engagement is about so much more than clicks and timestamps.
